speaker: interviewer
text: The date is March 31, 1997. Survivor Zoltan Wieder. Interviewer Naomi Rappaport. The city, Queens. The state, New York. The country, USA. Language, English. My name is Naomi Rappaport, N-A-O-M-I R-A-P-P-A-P-O-R-T. And today is March 31, 1997. I'm conducting an interview with Mr. Zoltan Wieder in New York-- in Queens, New York, USA.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning Mr. Wieder.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank you very much for welcoming us to your home and sharing your testimony with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a pleasure to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'd like to start with your name. Could you give us your name and spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My name Zoltan Wieder, Z-O-L-T-A-N W-I-E-D-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth name was Zoltan Wieder, but it was spelled differently. It was spelled with a V, V-I-D-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have any Hebrew names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my Hebrew name is Isroel. And they used to call me, at home, when I was a child, S'ruku.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And how did you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the Hebrew name I spelled [SPELLING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: The nickname, S-R-U-C-U.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And any other names at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date is 25 July, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Baia-Sprie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's in Romanian. And in Hungarian, they called it Felsobanya.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. But how do you spell Baia-Sprie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baia-Sprie? B-A-I-A and S-P-R-I-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was in what country when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was Baia-Sprie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baia-Sprie was a small town. Actually, it was a city. It has about 5,000 population.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small mining town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish population were-- there were there about 300 people. And we had only one synagogue. And the most of the-- of the-- everybody knew everybody in that city. It was a small town. And the relations were very-- very good between the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of mining town was it? What did it in mine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they mined there gold, silver, iron, and zinc and chrome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Exactly where is Baia-Sprie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baia-Sprie is-- is in the northern part of Romania, which is Transylvania. It's a province of Romania. It's actually the northern part of Transylvania. It's very close-- used to be the-- the prior border was Czechoslovakia. And now it's Ukraine. It's about 50 miles from Ukraine. It's Sighet, which is-- which is right the border town of Ukraine. It's-- in Romania, you call it Sighetu Marmatiei. That's-- it's about 35 miles from Baia-Sprie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the mine? Where were the mines?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mines were right in the town of Baia-Sprie. It-- right, actually, not in the center, but in the northern part of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm. Did your parents always live in Baia-Sprie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my parents always lived in Baia-Sprie. My father was born in Baia-Sprie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Samuel Wieder.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother's name was Regina. Her maiden name was Mendel. She was born in another town called, in Romanian, [ROMANIAN] or in Hungarian, [HUNGARIAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was about-- the-- about 20 miles from Zalau, which was the biggest town near there. And it was about 50, 60 miles from Baia-Sprie, south of Baia-Sprie.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do in--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Baia-Sprie?

speaker: interviewee
text: --he had a-- a general store, a small store. And I know I-- I was born in '25. So I heard, you know, in '29 when it was the depression here, they had big problems there too. They called it [NON-ENGLISH] And he lost-- because they sold in the general store based on-- on credit. You know, people paid once a month. And they lost a lot of money because they couldn't pay.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he struggled after that with-- with his store. He didn't have enough money to buy merchandise. And he had to go to work also, beside, in-- in the forest industry. He worked for the-- for a company. He was-- What did he do for--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was managing-- he was a-- a forest technician also. So he worked-- he was managing people in the work, organize the work, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. They-- what did-- what did they do with the lumber?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they cut trees in the-- in the forest. And they-- and they produced wood. And because everybody was there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cooking with-- with-- or-- or heating the homes with wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all wood-burn stones-- stoves. And beside, they produce also coal, coal which was used for the steel industry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. What did the Jewish population do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish population, in general, there were about 300 people, like I said, including the children. They were pretty poor, most of them. There were a few families in better conditions, some middle class. Like I say, my-- my family was also some middle-class people. But they were struggling. They were working very hard. And some, they had some stores. Others, they worked in different professions-- carpenters, tailors. They-- some, they worked in agriculture. Some, they worked with-- they had horses and little wagons. And that was, in general, the work what they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm. In Baia-Sprie, in the town itself, where did the Jews live?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews lived-- some of them lived in the middle of the town, in the center of the town, actually, because it was one main street. The whole town was a big main-- a long main street. And there were some small streets besides. Some, they lived on that main street. And some, they lived in different small streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we lived in-- in the center of the town, but on a-- on a-- not on that main street, on a-- on a secondary street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall what your house looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I recall very vividly. Our house was built right on-- on a little river. They called it Sasar in Romanian, or-- or [HUNGARIAN] in Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we looked from the window, and we had the river right-- right on our-- right near our house. And we had the store in front of the-- in front of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the store--

speaker: interviewee
text: The store in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Part of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of the house, the store, a small store. And we had our bedroom there. And we had a living room. And we had another room where we-- I-- that was our room, my-- my brothers and myself. We were four children. My brother, myself--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name was Adolf. And his Hebrew name was [NON-ENGLISH]. And he was four years older than me. He was born in 1921, May 10th. And I had two sisters. My one sister, her-- her name-- well, she was two years younger than me. Her Hebrew name was-- Yiddish name-- Blima. And her official name was Wilhelmina. And I had a smaller sister. She was four years younger than me. She was born in '29. Her name was Khana. And Anna in-- in Hungarian or Romanian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were living-- we were living in that-- we had only, actually, two rooms beside the living room. And it was pretty crowded. And the bathroom was outside. Not-- we didn't have inside. We had running water and-- at that time. And I remember, we had there, if somebody needed the bathroom, they had to go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: The house, however, had running water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had running water.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about electricity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had electricity too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it your own home?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was our own home, yeah. And that was my father got it from his father and-- and helping. And-- and he built it up a little bit. And so that was our own home. It-- it was considered a-- a pretty big house there, you know, at that time. And we had a yard in the home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you grow anything in the yard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had flowers. And beautiful-- my-- my mother loved flowers. And she worked all the time with her flowers. We had-- [CRYING] [SOBBING] And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and we did, because there were wood stoves there, so my brother, myself, we always chopped wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did you have animals in your yard? Did you keep any kind of animals.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had-- we have-- we had cats.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a dog. But there you kept the dog in the-- in the yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had two, three cats all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you living in an area that was Jewish? Or were-- did you have other neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: There it wasn't like here-- you have a Jewish area or not. That was-- you know, there were some Jews there, around there. There were some other people around there, gentile people, Hungarians, because it was a-- there were-- this town was mostly Hungarians. And the Romanian-- there lived some Romanian peoples too. But the Romanians mostly around the-- around the town in small-- and other religious. But most of the townspeople were Hungarians, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship with the Hungarian population?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we had with the population-- we had very good relationships in the beginning. And potentially started when-- in 19-- it was still Romania in 1938 when the-- when the Guardists came, the-- the Iron Guard came to power in Romania. It was a lot-- a lot of antisemitism therein. And I remember, in 1938, they-- as soon as they came to power, they gave-- they gave out a decree. We have to open the store in Shabbat. So that was a very big thing because my parents were religious. And so they had to open. And-- and it was only one week because that government fall after two weeks. And the King Carol-- Carol was the king of Romania at that time. He took over the government at that time. And so they fell. But it was a lot of anti-Semitism. And I remember my teachers was a family-- [NON-ENGLISH]. That was their name. And he was a [NON-ENGLISH]. That was also the-- from-- connected with Iron Guard. But he was nicer to us because we grew up there. They grew up there. But I remember he went to rallies, to Cluj and other parts, you know. And-- and they-- and at rallies, they incited the people and against the Jews, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Before the Iron Cross and before '38, what was the relationship between-- did you have non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had non-Jewish friends in the school. And we were very good friends. We were good friends. And I remember I had a very good friend. And he was in the same school with me. And when the-- when they took us out from-- from-- to the ghetto from-- from our town with the trucks, he didn't-- I saw him. He didn't look at me. So he changed completely. And some of them, they did change, you know, at that time. But in the beginning, I had-- in the school, we had very good relations. I had a lot of friends, non-Jewish too. But I had-- my best friends were Jewish boys. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you visit each other's homes, the non-Jewish homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jewish homes? Yes, we did visit non-Jewish homes too. And see, we had-- this was a small town. I had relatives there. I had my two uncles, two aunts there that were living there, my grandfather, who died in '36. So I knew him when I was a small child. Yeah, I remember he was-- he came-- he left-- he-- he was living next-- next house to us. And every morning he was coming. And to my younger sister, he was bringing cake. And he was widower, because I didn't know-- I didn't know my grandmother. She died before I was born. And he had a housekeeper. And-- and she-- she baked. And he always brought, in the morning, cake. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: [CRYING] Hm. Did-- what kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- [COUGHING] I went to the school that was only one school in the town, public school, naturally. And it was in the middle of the town. It was called [NON-ENGLISH], a primary school. It was a primary school, [NON-ENGLISH] Baia-Sprie. And I did there seven years, for seven years, including junior high-- eight years-- seven years. There were seven, actually, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Who were the teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The teachers were the Romanians. And some Hungarian teachers also. But they teach in the Romanian school. And they were nice, very nice. And I remember we-- we had some exams when it was in '38, in that time. And they put some exams on-- on a Saturday, on a Shabbat. And it was a big problem for me to go in and do the exams in Shabbes and write Shabbes. And one of the teachers, who was my teacher actually-- and she let me out to give the exam on another day. So there were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just you, or all the Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Me-- yeah, other Jewish boys also--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Boys and girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Were you-- was it coed? Were girls and boys in the same class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Coed, coed. Yeah, in the same class, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the percentage of kids in your class that were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, there were a few. Could be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe-- maybe 5%.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jewish teachers in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no Jewish teachers at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm. What language did they speak in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my house, they spoke Hungarian because my parents grew up in Hungary. At that time, it was Hungary, before-- before 1918. So they grew up in there. Their school's done in-- in Hungarian. And we spoke Yiddish too. So we spoke Hungarian and Yiddish in the house. Everybody spoke Yiddish, my-- my brother, my sisters. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- or, actually, my native language was Hungarian, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: At school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the school we spoke Romanian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Romanian. How were you dressed in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the school, we were dressed short pants because you didn't get long pants until you were 13, bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just Jewish kids wore short pants?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish-- no, everybody wore--

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody wore short--

speaker: interviewee
text: All the children wore short pants. Children below 12 or 13 years old, everybody wore short pants. And in the-- in the winter, we had some-- they called it some English pants that-- golf pants, like, they call-- golf pants, like they are here. And but we didn't have long pants when we were children. And we didn't have uniforms. But everybody was dressed, you know, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have your head covered in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't allow this. No, I didn't have my head-- we-- we didn't-- we didn't wear Yarmulkes there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hats?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not hats in the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. How about your father? How was he dressed?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was dressed regular business suit, a regular suit. And especially in the-- in the store, he was in a working suit. And he had a hat or a cap. And we wear caps actually, not in the school, but on the street. And when-- when we were younger, we wore caps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, she was dressed also. She-- you know, she helped in the store. But she was also-- you know, we were four children. She was doing the housekeeping and the cleaning and everything else. And she was very good cook. And that-- Friday morning, when I woke up, she was already cooking Danish and all kind of cakes and challah. She made the challah. She was doing it. And-- and we had a bakery right near our house. So we took there the challah. And they baked it for Shabbes and the [NON-ENGLISH]. And during the week, Monday she cooked bread. She baked bread. She had a variety of big bread. And they-- we took it to the bakery. They did it there. And-- and then the bread-- we took it back home. We had bread for a whole week.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: A big bread. It was tremendous, such a big bread. We had this-- we had it for a whole week.

speaker: interviewer
text: White, black, what kind of bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a white-- white, brownish, you know, because everybody was buying flour. We sold flour too in the store. And they took it home. And they made bread with-- with potatoes. They put in potatoes at that time. And no eggs in the bread. The challah, they did. And we had always, Tuesday morning, beginning Tuesday morning, fresh bread. We had also like a pizza type-- they did it. They called it langos, which-- which was a-- like a pita.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Flat-- like a flat bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: Flat, flat, yeah. Oh, we loved it. Tuesday morning, we got that fresh one. We loved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have her head covered?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she had a [NON-ENGLISH]. She had a-- a sheitel, a wig. She wear the wig. But otherwise she had covered her head.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. To go back to your school, what kind of games were played with these schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the school games? I tell you, every hour we had a few-- we had 5-- 10 minutes we had recreation there. We went out to the yard. And we played-- we played soccer. We did some gym, some gymnastics. But it was nothing with-- you didn't have a room, gymnastic room or something. Even-- even to get a ball was a big thing there in the school, you know, to play. So we played in the-- in the yard there for 10 minutes, and we went back to classes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Discipline was great, you know? You couldn't-- you looked up to your teacher like you were looking to-- to-- to God. You know, nobody was-- had a word in the school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and even-- there were corporal punishment. If somebody was bad, they were-- they were hitting them. They-- they got hit. But discipline was-- everybody was, you know, listening to the teacher. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did all the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --there were no talking back or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did all the kids play together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we played-- we played in groups, or sometimes together. And some people had different types of-- some played soccer. The girls didn't, you know. So they played something else, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any personal experience as a child in school with antisemitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually--

speaker: interviewer
text: In school.

speaker: interviewee
text: In school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or on the way home and back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had sometimes. They called me a Jew or something, you know? But very little there in the town until 1940 when the Hungarian came in. Then it started big antisemitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But before-- because we-- we knew each other and-- and we were really friends, considered friends. And-- and we didn't have too much. We had in other places. Like when I went to [ROMANIAN], to Cluj, that-- when they-- you know, they came after me or they tried to-- to hit me, or whatever, you know? And-- and screaming, 'Jew,' 'dirty Jew,' whatever, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was between '40 and '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that was later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the kehillah like, the Jewish kehillah?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kehillah, it was-- the Jewish kehillah was-- it was an Orthodox kehillah. It was a nice synagogue, a pretty big one. And the women were upstairs and-- and the men downstairs. [COUGHS] They had the ritual bath in the-- [COUGHS] --in the yard. And we went to, every morning and evening-- actually, we went to school, we couldn't go. We went-- they woke us up early morning. We went to the cheder to-- we were down there in the morning. And then we went to school. The public-- the public school started at 8 o'clock. So they woke us up about 6 o'clock. And we went to-- as young little children, we went to the cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the cheder, we--

speaker: interviewer
text: You and who else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Only the boys, not the girls. And from there, we went to the school, and until 1 o'clock. School was from 8:00 to 1:00. And at 1 o'clock, we went home. Everybody was eating lunch. Lunch was the main, the main course. That was the dinner, actually, at 1 o'clock. And everybody was sitting down to the table, the children. My father, if he was out in the-- in the forest-- he went out sometimes 20 miles or 10 miles, you know, to work. And he had his job-- or when he wasn't in the store. So otherwise, everybody was eating at 1 o'clock together. We had lunch. And at 2 o'clock, we went-- I went to the cheder back. And I was there until about 6 o'clock. And then I went home. And I-- I had to do my homework in the evening. And in the evening, we had only a cup of coffee or some bread with water, something like this. It was a light food in the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food for lunch, the-- the main meal?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the main dinner-- the main lunch was my mother cooked. And she had pretty-- pretty well-established. Like, Monday we had always dairy, dairy. We had soup. We had-- we had sour cream soup. And we had pasta with cheese. Or we had blintzes. And that was always Monday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Tuesday she did some other vegetable soup and-- and some pasta or something. And Wednesday, the same thing. And then Thursday she did-- that was a-- beans. There was a bean soup and, of course, some beans made up with some-- with some hamburgers, right? She made hamburgers too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, meat we had sometimes Thursday a little bit. But we had-- for Saturday we had meat. She bought meat from the butcher shop. And-- and we had some chicken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she cooked for Shabbes. Shabbes we had a better-- a better supper. We had always a Friday night supper. And-- and she had chicken soup. She had meat. She had, like, gefilte fish, but that was not from-- she was doing it from meat. It was a false-- they called it false fish. And it was very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. OK, we're going to have to change our tape now. OK, just--
